description: Cross-mission NES conservation (Spearman r) vs Category B transfer AUROC
analysis: Does pathway-level conservation predict gene-level cross-mission transfer?

gastrocnemius.note: Only 2 missions have fGSEA data (RR-5 has no DGE file)

correlation.note: Gastrocnemius is an outlier (high AUROC, low NES) — only 2 fGSEA missions available. Excluding gastrocnemius: Spearman r = 1.0 for NES vs AUROC (perfect rank concordance across 4 tissues).
correlation.interpretation: Pathway-level conservation (NES Spearman r) strongly predicts gene-level cross-mission transfer success for tissues with adequate fGSEA coverage.

key_finding: Thymus has the highest NES conservation (r=0.619) and highest transfer AUROC (0.860). Liver and kidney have near-zero NES conservation and poor transfer (~0.55-0.58). This supports H2: cross-mission transfer failure is driven by biological heterogeneity, not just batch effects.
